**Critique of eval_scenario_766: CNN vs. Decision Tree for Multi-Modal Mechanical Failure Detection**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting constitutes target-aware data leakage.**
The design explicitly states that "all transformers (audio MFCC extraction parameters, thermal image normalization statistics, video frame standardization, and any scaling or imputation) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting." This is not a stable-parameter safeguard — it is leakage. When preprocessing statistics (means, standard deviations, spectral normalization parameters) are computed on data that includes the test and validation sets, those held-out sets are no longer independent. The CNN and, critically, the Random Forest baseline both receive features whose normalization is contaminated by held-out distribution knowledge. In high-dimensional sensor data with distributional heterogeneity across machines and time periods, test-set statistics can differ meaningfully from training-set statistics. Fitting on the full dataset inflates the apparent stability of features and can materially shift test-set performance estimates. The claim that this approach guarantees "stable estimates" is not a methodological principle — it is a rationalization for a practice that breaks statistical independence. Evidence either way: rerun the experiment with preprocessing fit exclusively on the training fold. If test AUC shifts more than 1–2 points, the leakage was non-trivial.

**Issue 2: Stratified random split in a time-series industrial failure dataset breaks temporal causality and induces future leakage.**
The design uses stratified random sampling with machine-level stratification rather than a temporal split. In a predictive maintenance setting, the deployment question is: can the model predict failures from signals that precede them? Random shuffling of a 12-month record violates this causal structure. A training set that contains data from months 11–12 and a test set that contains data from months 1–2 means the model trains on future information when deployed. The claim that "stratification across all months ensures that the positive class rate is consistent" does not address the temporal leakage problem — it only addresses class balance, which is a separate concern. The practical consequence: estimated F1 scores will be optimistic relative to actual prospective performance. For a system designed to detect failures in future operation, a temporal holdout (train on months 1–10, validate on 11, test on 12) is the only valid evaluation. Evidence: compare model performance under temporal vs. random split. If the gap exceeds 5 points in F1, the random split is substantially inflating the estimate.

**Issue 3: The Random Forest receives CNN-derived preprocessing artifacts, not a matched baseline.**
The Random Forest baseline is trained on the same feature set as the CNN — specifically, 5-minute rolling window aggregates (mean, max, std) of MFCC, thermal statistics, and optical flow magnitude. But the CNN operates on raw spectrograms, thermal image sequences, and optical flow frames directly, not these aggregated statistics. The feature set described in section 3 (aggregate statistics per modality) appears designed for the Random Forest, while the CNN architecture described in section 4 (three convolutional pathways for audio spectrograms, thermal sequences, optical flow) takes raw sequences. If the CNN operates on raw inputs and the Random Forest on hand-crafted aggregates, the experiment is not measuring "architecture advantage" — it is confounding architecture with feature representation. Any CNN advantage could be due to richer input representation rather than model capacity. Evidence: train both models on the same feature type (either both on aggregates, or both on raw sequences via a flattened representation for the RF) and measure the residual gap.

**Issue 4: The F1 macro-average primary metric is miscalibrated for the stated deployment objective.**
The design justifies macro-averaged F1 as balancing false positives (unnecessary maintenance) and false negatives (missed failures). But macro-averaging treats both classes equally. In a manufacturing context where unplanned downtime is operationally catastrophic and missed failure costs 10–50x unnecessary maintenance, the correct metric is recall-weighted or a cost-sensitive metric. Macro-F1 penalizes false alarms as heavily as missed failures. The design acknowledges asymmetric costs in the text but then selects a symmetric metric. This is an internal contradiction. A model that achieves high precision but misses 40% of failures could outperform a higher-recall model on macro-F1. The per-class F1 and ROC-AUC listed as secondary metrics are better aligned with the stated objective but are demoted. Evidence: compare model ranking under macro-F1 vs. recall@0.9-precision and determine if the CNN vs. RF ordering changes.

**Issue 5: Single-facility, single-year scope combined with machine-type heterogeneity creates undetected cluster leakage.**
The 100,000 maintenance events across "multiple machines" at a single facility create a situation where many machines have repeated events in both training and test sets under random splitting. Even with machine-level stratification, the model trains on earlier events for a given machine and evaluates on later events — but under random splitting, "later" events for machine A may appear in the training set while "earlier" events appear in the test set. This produces within-machine temporal leakage because the stratification is by machine identity, not by machine-time ordering. A machine with 200 maintenance events will contribute roughly 134 to training and 34 to each of validation and test, but if selected randomly, some of those 34 test events may precede their 134 training counterparts chronologically. Evidence: audit the timestamp distribution of training vs. test events per machine to confirm whether any test event precedes its paired training events.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_776: CNN for Diabetic Retinopathy Severity Prediction**

**Issue 1: Splitting by image rather than by patient allows patient-level data leakage.**
The design partitions using stratified random sampling "on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID." This is an explicit acknowledgment that multiple images from the same patient may appear in different splits. In a longitudinal diabetic retinopathy dataset, a patient with moderate retinopathy at baseline and severe retinopathy at follow-up can have one image in training and another in the test set. Because retinopathy severity is correlated within patients (same underlying pathophysiology, same vascular anatomy, similar image characteristics), the model can partially memorize patient-specific features seen during training and exploit them at test time. This is patient-level leakage — a well-documented failure mode in medical imaging ML. The design explicitly chooses not to split by patient and provides a rationale about class ratio stability, but this rationale does not address the leakage. Evidence: split by patient ID and compare test AUROC. A drop of more than 2–3 AUROC points would indicate the original design was exploiting patient-level memorization.

**Issue 2: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting introduces leakage.**
The design states that "all image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset." For a retinal image classification task, per-channel standardization statistics derived from all 5,000 images will encode test-set distributional information into the preprocessing pipeline. If the test set is from a particular center with systematically different image brightness or contrast, the global statistics "borrow" information from that center's images during preprocessing, and the model's normalization is no longer independent of the test distribution. In a multi-center dataset with imaging protocol heterogeneity, this matters more than in a single-site homogeneous dataset. The design acknowledges center variation as a key concern; fitting preprocessing globally undercuts that concern. Evidence: fit preprocessing on training images only and measure whether center-specific performance changes.

**Issue 3: Comparing CNN to expert consensus grading as the baseline misrepresents the comparison.**
The design proposes the "consensus expert grading system currently used operationally" as the baseline. This is not equivalent to training a logistic regression or classical ML model — it is comparing a learned model against a process that already defines the ground truth. The labels are assigned by expert graders; using those same graders as the "baseline" creates circular logic: the experts are simultaneously the labelers and the comparator. Any CNN trained to predict the expert labels will, by construction, tend to agree with the experts on easy cases and diverge on hard cases — which is exactly what is captured by inter-rater reliability (kappa). The CNN's AUROC versus experts is measuring disagreement with the labeling process, not improvement over clinical practice. The correct baseline is a simpler algorithmic model (e.g., logistic regression on hand-crafted features, or a prior-generation CNN) — not the expert system that generated the labels. Evidence: add a k-nearest-neighbor or logistic regression baseline on extracted image features and compare all three: CNN, LR, expert system.

**Issue 4: Macro-AUROC on four severity grades masks performance on the clinically critical minority class.**
The design uses macro-averaged AUROC across four severity grades (no retinopathy: ~65%, mild: ~20%, moderate: ~10%, severe: ~5%). With severe retinopathy at 5% prevalence, the macro-average weights that class's AUROC equally with the 65%-prevalence no-retinopathy class. If the CNN performs at 0.95 AUROC on the dominant class and 0.60 on severe retinopathy, macro-AUROC of 0.80 looks acceptable but the model has failed on the clinically urgent case (severe retinopathy requiring immediate intervention). Per-class F1 is listed as a secondary metric, but given the clinical stakes of misclassifying severe cases, this should drive model selection, not be relegated to a secondary report. Evidence: check whether model selection by macro-AUROC produces a different top model than selection by severe-class AUROC or sensitivity at fixed specificity for the severe class.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_704: Multi-Task Deep Learning vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split in temporal EHR data enables anachronistic training — the model learns from the future.**
The design uses stratified random sampling "across all available months, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set." In hospital readmission prediction, patient encounters are embedded in time. A model trained on a randomly selected 85% of 60-month EHR data will train on patient records from months 50–60 and be evaluated on patient records from months 1–5. In deployment, no such future information exists. The model will implicitly learn temporal patterns (e.g., changes in discharge protocols, coding practices, drug availability) that corrupt its generalization to genuinely future patients. This is particularly acute with clinical BERT embeddings, which capture textual patterns in discharge summaries — if documentation style changed over the 60-month window, the model trains on the evolved style and evaluates on both. Evidence: compare AUROC under temporal split (train months 1–48, validate 49–54, test 55–60) versus the random split used. If the temporal split shows a performance drop of more than 3 points AUROC, the random split was producing inflated estimates.

**Issue 2: The multi-task auxiliary objective (LOS regression) introduces a target-correlated training signal that may artificially inflate readmission performance.**
The multi-task model shares encoder layers between the readmission classification task and a length-of-stay regression auxiliary task. LOS and 30-day readmission are correlated — patients with longer hospital stays tend to be sicker and more likely to be readmitted. This means the shared encoder that learns to predict LOS will encode features predictive of readmission. The design frames this as "regularization," but the mechanism is richer than regularization: the auxiliary task actively shapes the representation toward readmission-relevant features. The problem is that the logistic regression baseline has no such auxiliary signal. The comparison is then not "architecture vs. architecture" but rather "multi-task learning with auxiliary signal vs. single-task learning without it." To test whether the multi-task architecture per se provides benefit — rather than just the auxiliary label — the design needs a third condition: multi-task model trained without the auxiliary task (identical architecture, single head). Evidence: ablate the auxiliary LOS head and measure the readmission AUROC delta.

**Issue 3: AUROC is insensitive to the operational threshold decision that determines clinical utility.**
The design selects AUROC as the primary metric with the justification that it is "insensitive to class imbalance and threshold choice." In a hospital readmission intervention context, the operational question is: which patients receive a resource-intensive post-discharge intervention? This requires a threshold decision. AUROC measures ranking ability across all thresholds but says nothing about whether any particular operating point is clinically useful. A model with AUROC 0.80 and precision of 12% at the threshold used operationally is not clinically useful even if it ranks well. The design does include a secondary metric (sensitivity/specificity at a "clinically-calibrated operating point" targeting ≥90% sensitivity), but uses the validation set to calibrate this threshold — and then reports it on the test set without treating threshold selection as part of the evaluation. The threshold selection on validation, then test evaluation with that threshold, is correct procedurally, but this metric should be primary given the operational objective. Evidence: report the positive predictive value at the 90%-sensitivity operating point selected on validation, and determine if the multi-task model's PPV is meaningfully higher than the logistic regression's — this is the metric that determines whether deployment is feasible.

**Issue 4: TF-IDF + clinical BERT concatenation introduces collinearity between the two text representations that degrades both models equally.**
The feature engineering section constructs two text representations — TF-IDF bag-of-words (500 terms) and clinical BERT embeddings (768 dimensions) — and concatenates them. These two representations are not orthogonal: TF-IDF term presence is correlated with BERT token frequencies. Concatenating redundant high-dimensional features increases the effective feature space dimensionality without proportionally increasing signal, which is particularly harmful for logistic regression (collinearity inflates variance of coefficients). However, the logistic regression baseline receives both the TF-IDF and BERT concatenation while it is designed as a "structured feature" model. If clinical BERT embeddings alone contain sufficient signal, TF-IDF adds noise to the logistic regression input, deflating its performance relative to a logistic regression that uses only the more informative BERT embeddings. The design may be inadvertently handicapping the baseline. Evidence: compare logistic regression on (TF-IDF + BERT) vs. logistic regression on (BERT only) on the validation set. If BERT-only LR outperforms the concatenation, the feature engineering is disadvantaging the baseline.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_770: Multi-Task Learning for Heart Failure Readmission and LOS Prediction**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split in longitudinal heart failure data induces patient-level leakage and temporal leakage simultaneously.**
The design states "the data is stratified using a random split across the entire dataset," covering 36 months of data and 5,000–7,000 hospitalizations across approximately 5,000 patients. Given the cohort definition ("all patients with ICD-10 I50.x"), many patients have multiple hospitalizations (the design acknowledges readmissions as part of the cohort). Under a random split, patient A's hospitalization in month 1 may be in the test set while their hospitalization in month 30 is in the training set. This creates two simultaneous leakage mechanisms: (a) within-patient leakage because the model sees the patient's features at a later time point during training and evaluates on an earlier time point, and (b) temporal leakage because training data from late in the study period contaminates the evaluation of data from early in the study period. A temporal patient-level split (split by earliest hospitalization date per patient, holding out the most recent cohort as test) is the correct design for this setting. Evidence: compare model performance under temporal patient-level split vs. the proposed random split. A significant degradation under the temporal split indicates the random split was exploiting these leakage mechanisms.

**Issue 2: The composite primary metric combines rank-based and scale-based measures in an arbitrary weighting.**
The primary metric is defined as (Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4). This formulation is problematic on multiple levels. First, AUC-ROC is bounded [0,1] and LoS RMSE rank is not in the same units — the design converts RMSE to a rank but does not specify how the rank is computed (rank among what reference distribution?). If LoS RMSE ranks are derived from model-specific performance across validation folds, this requires a reference set of models, which is unavailable at test time. Second, the 0.6/0.4 weighting is arbitrary — the design claims it "reflects clinical priority" but provides no quantitative justification. A weighting of 0.9/0.1 or 0.5/0.5 would reflect equally defensible clinical priorities. Third, model selection on this composite metric during hyperparameter tuning will bias the alpha parameter (loss weighting) toward the combination that maximizes this composite, not the combination that independently maximizes either clinical outcome. Evidence: report results on the two component metrics independently under different alpha values and determine whether the composite optimum corresponds to the clinical optimum for readmission alone.

**Issue 3: The separate-model baseline has a structural advantage the multi-task model lacks: independent optimization per task.**
The baseline consists of two independently trained models (logistic regression for readmission, linear regression for LoS). Each baseline model is optimized exclusively for its own task using the validation criterion most relevant to that task. The multi-task model must jointly optimize a weighted sum of two losses, and the alpha parameter (α ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}) is tuned on the composite metric — meaning neither task receives fully uncompromised optimization. The comparison is therefore not "multi-task architecture vs. single-task architecture" but rather "jointly constrained optimization vs. independently unconstrained optimization." If the multi-task model achieves lower readmission AUC than the baseline LR, this could reflect that the LoS auxiliary task is degrading the readmission representation rather than that multi-task learning is inferior. The design does not test this because there is no ablation condition that runs the multi-task architecture as a single-task model. Evidence: add a third condition — the identical multi-task neural architecture trained with alpha=1.0 (readmission only, no LoS head) — and compare its readmission AUC to both the multi-task and logistic regression.

**Issue 4: Stratification by hospital unit during train/val/test splits does not control for physician-level confounding in LOS prediction.**
The design proposes stratification by "hospital unit and admitting team during train/val/test splits" and acknowledges physician/protocol heterogeneity as a confound. However, stratified random sampling by unit distributes unit representation equally across splits but does not ensure that individual physicians appear equally across splits. If a single high-volume cardiologist with systematically longer LOS appears predominantly in the training set (because their patients are clustered in time and the random split draws heavily from their cluster), LOS predictions will be calibrated to their practice pattern, not the general unit pattern. Temporal clustering of physician practice is common in hospital data. Evidence: report the physician-level Gini coefficient of case distribution across train/test splits to determine whether physician clustering is materially unequal despite unit stratification.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_764: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split of 36-month EHR data with acknowledged temporal variation is methodologically incoherent.**
Section 2 explicitly states "While the introduction of future data might occur, the benefit of consistent class distribution is prioritized." This is not a methodological trade-off — it is a concession that the design is conducting prospective-sounding prediction research with a non-prospective evaluation. In deployment, the model will be applied to patients discharged after the training period ends. The random split produces a test set drawn from months 1–36 concurrently with the training set, which means the evaluation simulates no real deployment scenario. The design acknowledges this is a problem and proceeds anyway. The quarterly stratification analysis mentioned in section 7 would detect drift, but it cannot retroactively repair the non-temporal split. Any performance estimate from this design overstates likely deployment performance because temporal distribution shift is not evaluated. Evidence: implement a temporal split (first 30 months training, next 3 validation, last 3 test) and compare AUROC. If the temporal split AUROC is more than 3 points lower, the random split was producing misleading estimates.

**Issue 2: Retraining on combined training + validation data after model selection inflates test performance.**
Section 6 states: "After selection, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters. This maximizes training data while maintaining an unbiased test estimate." The claim of "unbiased test estimate" is incorrect. When a model is retrained on combined train + validation using hyperparameters selected by validation performance, the final model has effectively seen the validation distribution during training (the hyperparameters that minimize validation loss are tuned to validation characteristics). Retraining on combined data is appropriate when the goal is deployment, but the test set performance of the retrained model is no longer a valid bound on generalization error because the validation set, which informed hyperparameter selection, is now in the training data. The correct procedure for an unbiased test estimate is to evaluate the model trained exclusively on the training set (the configuration selected by validation), not the model retrained on train + validation. Evidence: compare test AUROC of the training-set-only model vs. the train+validation retrained model. If the retrained model is more than 1 point higher, the retraining step is inflating the test estimate.

**Issue 3: The transformer architecture uses frozen Clinical BERT embeddings concatenated with tabular embeddings, passed through a transformer encoder — but the self-attention mechanism has no natural sequence to attend over.**
The architecture concatenates a 128-dim note embedding and a 64-dim tabular embedding, then passes the combined vector through "a 2-layer transformer encoder (8 attention heads, 256 hidden units)." Transformer encoders apply self-attention over token sequences. A single 192-dimensional concatenated vector is not a sequence — it is a single vector. Self-attention over a single vector with no positional structure reduces to a weighted linear projection, which is equivalent to a dense layer. The claimed advantage of the transformer ("capture interactions" between note and tabular features) is a standard cross-modal fusion mechanism available via simple concatenation + dense layers. The transformer encoder adds no architectural capacity that a two-layer MLP would not provide for this input structure. If the transformer performs better than logistic regression, it may be due to the multi-layer nonlinearity rather than attention. This misconfiguration could produce plausible-looking results (the architecture runs without errors) while the attention mechanism is degenerate. Evidence: replace the transformer encoder with an equivalent-parameter dense layer and compare performance. If there is no significant difference, the transformer architecture is doing no more than an MLP.

**Issue 4: Macro-averaged vs. weighted F1 selection is ambiguous and consequential for model comparison.**
The design states the primary metric is "F1-score (macro-averaged or weighted)" — presenting both options without resolving which is primary. In an imbalanced binary classification task (readmission rate ~15–25%), macro-averaged F1 gives equal weight to both classes and is dominated by performance on the majority negative class, while weighted F1 weights by class prevalence. The choice between these produces meaningfully different model rankings in imbalanced settings. If macro-F1 is used, a model that perfectly predicts all negatives but misses all positives gets approximately 0.5 F1 (depending on threshold); if weighted F1 is used, it gets approximately 0.87. This ambiguity in the primary metric means the experiment's success criterion is undefined. Evidence: pre-register which F1 variant is primary and confirm that the model comparison ordering is consistent between macro- and weighted-F1 on the validation set.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_738: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for 30-Day Heart Failure Readmission Prediction**

**Issue 1: Structured feature preprocessing is fit on the entire dataset before splitting, contaminating the temporal split.**
The design correctly implements a temporal split (training months 1–36, validation 37–48, test 49–60) and is explicit about patient-level integrity. However, section 3 contradicts this for structured data: "All preprocessing transformers are designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting." This means z-score normalization parameters for admission vitals (BNP, creatinine, eGFR) and one-hot encoding vocabularies are computed using test-set observations — specifically, months 49–60 data — before the temporal holdout. For a 5-year clinical dataset, biomarker distributions can shift due to changes in lab equipment calibration, patient population changes, or updated normal ranges. Test-set BNP distributions inform the standardization parameters used during training. This selectively undermines the temporal split's primary purpose. Evidence: refit preprocessing on months 1–36 only, apply to months 37–60 without refitting, and measure whether test AUROC changes. If it drops more than 1 point, the global fitting was leaking test distribution information.

**Issue 2: The baseline (unimodal text-only) is not the correct comparator for the hypothesis being tested.**
The experiment's stated goal is to test whether multi-modal integration (text + images + structured data) outperforms unimodal text-only. The baseline is a text-only BioBERT + BiLSTM model. But adding structured data alone to the text model could explain all the gain — the experiment cannot separate the contribution of echocardiogram images from the contribution of structured tabular features (ejection fraction, BNP, medication classes). Echocardiograms are expensive, require specialized equipment, and are not available for all patients. If the multimodal gain is driven by structured tabular features rather than echocardiogram images, the clinical recommendation would be to use text + structured data (without imaging), not the full multimodal model. A three-way comparison (text-only, text + structured, text + structured + images) is required to isolate the imaging contribution. Evidence: compare the three conditions on validation AUROC. If text + structured matches the full multimodal model, echocardiogram images add no marginal value despite their cost.

**Issue 3: The "consistent across all subgroups" hypothesis support criterion is statistically undefined and almost certainly unfalsifiable.**
Section 7 states: "The hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups." Consistency across all six subgroup stratifications (age, sex, race, comorbidity quartile, treatment, temporal) is a conjunction of six conditions. Each subgroup AUROC estimate is noisier than the overall estimate. Requiring unanimous directional agreement across all subgroups sets a bar that is almost certainly not achievable in practice even if the model genuinely outperforms — because some subgroups will have small N and wide confidence intervals that cross zero. Conversely, a model that degrades on a critical subgroup (e.g., elderly patients with higher comorbidity burden) could still satisfy the criterion if the direction is positive. The criterion needs a minimum effect size per subgroup and a statistical test for interaction effects. Evidence: pre-specify the minimum detectable AUROC difference per subgroup given the expected subgroup sample sizes and determine how many of the six subgroups are adequately powered.

**Issue 4: AUROC as primary metric for a multi-modal model with incomplete imaging data may produce misleading estimates.**
The design acknowledges "documentation completeness: performance for complete vs. partial imaging availability" as a subgroup analysis. This implies some patients lack echocardiogram images. If the multimodal model is evaluated on all patients (including those without images) with imputed or zero-padded image representations, its AUROC is a mixture of performance with and without the imaging modality. A model that achieves high AUROC primarily by routing to text + structured features for patients without images — and uses images only for patients who have them — will appear to be a high-performing multimodal model when it is functioning as a mixed-modality model. The experiment should report AUROC separately for patients with complete modality data vs. those with missing images. Evidence: compare AUROC on the subgroup with complete echocardiogram coverage. If the multimodal model's AUROC advantage over the baseline is larger in that subgroup than in the full population, the imaging modality is providing genuine incremental value.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_735: RNN-Based Predictive Maintenance vs. Statistical Process Control**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split of 60 months of sequential sensor data destroys the temporal causal structure critical for predictive maintenance.**
The design uses "stratified random sampling across all available months, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set." A predictive maintenance model operates prospectively: at time T, predict failures within T+7 days. Under random splitting, a sensor window from month 55 may appear in the training set while a sensor window from month 10 appears in the test set. The RNN trains on later patterns and evaluates on earlier patterns, which is the inverse of deployment. Crucially, equipment degradation is monotonic over time — a machine in month 55 is older and more failure-prone than in month 10. A model that trains on degraded-machine sensor patterns and evaluates on fresher-machine patterns is evaluating under conditions systematically more favorable than deployment (the test set has less degradation). The description notes in section 6 that "the configuration maximizing validation F1 is selected" and validation is "months 37–48" — but this conflicts with section 2 which says stratified random sampling is used, not a temporal split. The design contains an internal contradiction between sections 2 and 6 regarding how splits are constructed. Evidence: resolve the contradiction by specifying whether months 37–48 are reserved as a contiguous validation block (temporal split) or are randomly sampled. If random sampling is used, switch to temporal and compare F1.

**Issue 2: The SPC baseline is not tuned to an equivalent operating point as the RNN, making F1 comparison invalid.**
The SPC baseline uses "a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3" with the explicit justification of "providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing to a specific time period." But the RNN is extensively tuned (grid search over learning rate, LSTM units, dropout, batch size on validation data). The SPC baseline operates at a fixed operating point chosen by convention, while the RNN operates at an optimized operating point. F1 is threshold-sensitive for both: the RNN's threshold is chosen to maximize validation F1, while the SPC's threshold is fixed at the 2-sigma convention. If the SPC threshold were tuned (e.g., by searching over control limit multipliers {1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0} on the validation set), its F1 could be substantially higher. The design explicitly rejects this tuning to avoid "over-optimizing to a specific time period," but that justification applies equally to the RNN. Evidence: tune the SPC control limit multiplier and violation threshold on the validation set and compare F1 to both the untuned SPC and the RNN. If tuned SPC narrows the gap, the comparison was unfair.

**Issue 3: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting leaks future degradation statistics into the normalization.**
The design states "all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split." Z-score scaling parameters (global mean and std for each sensor per machine) include data from the test period. For sensors measuring equipment degradation (vibration, temperature), the distribution in months 55–60 may be systematically higher than in months 1–12 due to equipment aging. Fitting global normalization on all 60 months means the training set's features are normalized using statistics that partially reflect the degraded test-period sensor behavior. This shifts the apparent feature distribution of training data toward the test distribution, leaking test-set distributional information into model training. Evidence: fit normalization only on training-period data and measure whether model performance changes. If rolling statistics for late-period test windows are normalized differently under training-only parameters, the leakage was material.

**Issue 4: Overlapping 7-day prediction windows at 1-minute resolution create massive label autocorrelation that inflates F1.**
Each sensor window is 1,008 observations (7 days at 1-minute resolution), and the label is whether failure occurs within 7 days after the window. Adjacent windows (offset by 1 minute) overlap by 1,007 observations and are almost certainly labeled identically. Under random sampling, adjacent windows will appear in both training and test sets, creating a situation where the test set contains near-identical copies of training examples. The RNN is not actually predicting on genuinely unseen conditions — it is recognizing previously-seen sensor patterns with minute perturbations. F1 estimates under this condition are not estimates of out-of-sample generalization; they are estimates of interpolation between near-duplicate examples. The design should stride windows by at least 1 day (1,440 observations) or use patient/equipment-level splits with temporal ordering. Evidence: measure the correlation between adjacent window labels and determine what fraction of test windows have a near-duplicate (>99% feature overlap) in the training set.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_773: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the complete dataset before splitting is leakage, contradicting the stated patient-level stratification goal.**
Section 4 states: "all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting. This approach provides consistent transformation across all data subsets." Fitting scalers and vocabulary builders on the full dataset means the test set's feature distributions inform the normalization applied during training. For a 5-year EHR dataset with temporal shifts in clinical coding (ICD-10 code adoption patterns, new drug entries), vocabulary built from all data will contain terms from test-period records that do not appear in training records. This gives the RNN access to the distribution of test-period clinical codes during training (through shared embedding spaces), which is leakage. The stated rationale — "consistency and stability" — is not a valid exception to train-set-only preprocessing. Evidence: fit preprocessing exclusively on training data, apply to validation and test without refitting, and measure whether PR-AUC on the validation set changes more than 1 point.

**Issue 2: The test set is accessed during hyperparameter selection to "fine-tune model architecture," which is test set contamination.**
Section 6 states: "After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed." This explicitly describes using the test set during model development. "Fine-tuning model architecture if discrepancies are observed" means that test set performance is feeding back into the architecture selection loop. This is not a "check" — it is test-set-guided model selection, which completely invalidates the test set as an unbiased performance estimator. Any reported test-set metrics after this process are optimistic relative to true out-of-sample performance. This is one of the most fundamental errors in ML methodology and is stated explicitly as a design choice. Evidence: this is not an empirical question — using the test set for architecture fine-tuning is definitionally invalidating. The design must be corrected before any results are interpretable.

**Issue 3: The RNN receives a richer feature set than the logistic regression baseline by design, making the comparison architecture plus feature set rather than architecture alone.**
The RNN uses visit sequences spanning 12 months, including admission/discharge dates, LOS, primary/secondary diagnosis codes, procedure codes, medication classes, and lab summaries — a temporal, multi-visit representation. The logistic regression baseline uses only "demographic and clinical summary features" from the index visit: comorbidity count, chronic condition count, prior hospitalization count, ED visit frequency, medication count, LOS, and primary diagnosis. The design explicitly excludes raw sequences from the logistic regression. This means the RNN has access to the full 12-month visit sequence while the logistic regression has only aggregated summaries. Any performance difference could reflect the richer feature representation (raw sequences vs. aggregates) rather than the architecture's ability to learn from sequences. A fair comparison would either give logistic regression the same aggregated features derived from the full 12-month sequence or test both models on the same raw sequence representation. Evidence: train logistic regression on the full set of sequence-derived features (all 12 months of diagnosis codes, procedure codes, etc., flattened) and compare to the RNN. If the feature-matched LR narrows the gap by more than 50%, the architecture comparison was confounded by feature representation.

**Issue 4: Patient-level stratification under random split does not prevent temporal leakage in a longitudinal cohort.**
The design claims patient-level stratification prevents "patient leakage" by ensuring each patient appears in only one split. For readmission prediction in a 5-year cohort, patients may have multiple admissions over the 5-year window. If the design selects only the index visit (as stated in section 2: "patients with fewer than one prior visit are excluded"), then each patient has exactly one record and patient-level stratification is equivalent to random splitting — no multi-event leakage is possible but the temporal leakage problem (training on future-dated records, evaluating on past-dated records) remains. If patients have multiple visits and only one is selected per patient, the selection criterion for which visit to include can introduce selection bias. The design does not specify how the index visit is selected when a patient has multiple admissions. Evidence: clarify the index visit selection criterion and check whether visit selection introduces systematic differences in patient severity between training and test splits.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_774: CNN for Knee Replacement Recovery Time Prediction**

**Issue 1: The proxy outcome (30-day pain reduction) does not match the stated hypothesis (recovery time prediction).**
The hypothesis tests whether a CNN predicts "knee replacement recovery time," but the outcome variable is "pain reduction 30 days post-surgery," described as "highly correlative with recovery speed." Pain reduction at 30 days is a surrogate endpoint, not a direct measure of recovery time. Correlation with recovery speed is not equivalence. Two models could rank identically on 30-day pain reduction but differ substantially in their rank ordering of actual recovery duration (e.g., time to return to normal gait, time to discontinue physiotherapy, time to achieve functional milestones). The hypothesis's claim rests on the surrogate's correlation with the true outcome being perfect — but if that correlation is less than 0.95, models optimized for the surrogate will not be optimal for the true endpoint. No correlation estimate between 30-day pain reduction and actual recovery time is provided or referenced. Evidence: collect actual recovery time data (even for a subsample) and compute the Spearman correlation between 30-day pain reduction and recovery time. If rho < 0.9, the proxy is not interchangeable with the true outcome.

**Issue 2: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting contaminates MRI normalization with test-set image statistics.**
The design states "all transformers are initially fitted on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly in model training and evaluation across all splits." For MRI scans, normalization involves computing intensity statistics (mean, standard deviation, potentially per-scanner parameters) across all 24 months of data. If MRI acquisition parameters varied across the 24-month period (new scanner, software update, protocol revision), the test-period scan statistics will influence the normalization applied to training scans. This is particularly problematic because ResNet-18 pretrained on ImageNet expects specific normalization ranges, and the model's performance on test MRIs depends on how well normalization brings test images into the expected distribution. Fitting on all images rather than training images only leaks test-set distributional information. Evidence: fit normalization on training MRIs only, apply to test MRIs, and check whether MAPE on validation changes materially.

**Issue 3: The comparison is between a CNN that receives MRI plus structured features vs. XGBoost that receives only structured features — this is a feature set comparison, not an architecture comparison.**
The CNN model receives MRI scans AND structured features (demographics, clinical history, surgical details), while the XGBoost baseline receives only structured features. The hypothesis is whether "the CNN outperforms gradient boosting with structured features alone." This framing acknowledges the design is testing feature set inclusion (imaging vs. no imaging) rather than architecture capacity. But the CNN also has a fundamentally different inductive bias for the structured features (concatenation after flattening feature maps) compared to XGBoost's tree-based feature interactions. Even if the CNN outperforms XGBoost, the attributable cause could be: (a) MRI features add genuine signal, (b) the deep learning architecture is better at structured feature interactions, or (c) the ResNet backbone encodes ImageNet priors that happen to be relevant. None of these can be distinguished without a third condition: an XGBoost model trained on CNN-extracted MRI features plus structured features. Evidence: run XGBoost on CNN-extracted image embeddings plus structured features. If this model matches the end-to-end CNN, the CNN's advantage is due to image features, not architecture.

**Issue 4: MAPE as primary metric is undefined when the true outcome can be near zero.**
The primary metric is Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). If 30-day pain reduction scores are on a scale that includes values near zero (e.g., a patient with 0.1 improvement out of 10), MAPE divides the absolute error by the true value, producing explosive error magnitudes for small true values. For a 24-month orthopedic dataset, some patients will have minimal pain reduction at 30 days (e.g., due to complications or high baseline pain tolerance), and MAPE will be dominated by these edge cases. The secondary metric listed as "mean absolute error (robustness to outliers)" contradicts the choice of MAPE as primary — MAE is more robust to outliers; MAPE is less robust to small denominators. The design should either specify a minimum threshold for the denominator in MAPE or adopt MAE as the primary metric. Evidence: examine the distribution of 30-day pain reduction scores in the dataset. If more than 5% of values are less than 0.5 on the pain scale, MAPE will be dominated by those cases and will not represent typical model behavior.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_752: Deep RL vs. Traditional Methods for Predictive Maintenance**

**Issue 1: The RL reward function is a hyperparameter of the model, not a fixed external specification — optimizing it on validation conflates policy learning with objective specification.**
The design tunes "reward weights" as part of hyperparameter search on the validation set. The reward function (penalizing false negatives heavily, unnecessary maintenance less heavily) determines what the RL policy learns to optimize. If the reward weights are tuned to maximize validation AUPRC, the policy is shaped by the validation set in two ways: (a) through hyperparameter search, and (b) through the reinforcement signal itself. This creates a feedback loop where the reward function that generates the policy's learning signal is selected by its performance on the validation set. This is conceptually different from tuning learning rate in supervised learning — changing the reward weights changes the optimization target, not just the speed of convergence. The test set evaluation is then measuring the performance of a policy trained with a reward function selected to be favorable on the validation distribution. Evidence: evaluate the RL policy trained with the default/domain-expert reward weights (not validation-tuned) on the test set and compare to the validation-tuned version. If there is a substantial gap, the reward tuning step is adapting the policy to the validation distribution rather than the true operational objective.

**Issue 2: AUPRC as the primary metric for an RL model that makes discrete actions (maintain/defer) is conceptually mismatched.**
AUPRC measures the area under the precision-recall curve, which is meaningful for probabilistic classifiers that produce a continuous score. Deep RL policy networks output action probabilities (or Q-values), not calibrated failure probabilities. Translating RL action probabilities to a precision-recall curve requires treating the probability of the "maintain" action as a failure score — but this probability reflects the policy's learned value function for that state, not the posterior probability of failure. A policy that has learned to "maintain" aggressively (high action probability) will appear to have high recall but may do so for reasons unrelated to failure risk. The logistic regression baseline produces calibrated failure probabilities and its precision-recall curve is interpretable. Comparing AUPRC between an RL policy and a logistic regression is comparing incommensurable quantities. Evidence: for the RL model, compute the actual failure rate among cases where the policy recommends maintenance (empirical precision) and compare to logistic regression precision at the same recall level. If the RL model's action probabilities don't correlate with empirical failure rates, AUPRC is measuring policy aggressiveness rather than failure discrimination.

**Issue 3: The RL evaluation on historical offline data does not reflect the online interaction nature of the system being simulated.**
Deep RL is typically evaluated in interactive environments where the agent's actions influence future states. In offline historical data, the RL agent's maintenance actions cannot change the equipment's degradation trajectory — the data was generated under a different maintenance policy. Evaluating an RL policy on historical logs using standard classification metrics (AUPRC, recall) implicitly assumes that the policy's counterfactual actions would have led to the same observed failure outcomes. But if the RL policy defers maintenance on equipment that was historically maintained before failure, the counterfactual failure outcome is unknown. Off-policy evaluation in RL is a hard problem that requires importance weighting or model-based simulation. The design does not address this and treats RL evaluation as equivalent to supervised classification evaluation, which is incorrect. Evidence: this is a structural flaw — the design cannot correctly evaluate an RL policy using historical observational data without off-policy correction. Either use a simulator or restrict the comparison to a supervised formulation.

**Issue 4: The 30-month training window for RL may be insufficient to learn state-value functions over multi-month equipment degradation trajectories.**
The design uses months 13–42 (30 months) for training and notes that "reinforcement learning models benefit from longer training windows to learn state-value functions over sufficient trajectories." With approximately 8–12% positive class prevalence and equipment-group stratification, the number of complete failure trajectories in the training set may be in the hundreds. RL policy gradient methods typically require many thousands of episodes to converge. If the average equipment lifecycle spans 6–18 months before failure, 30 months of training provides only 2–5 full degradation cycles per equipment unit. This is likely insufficient for stable Q-value or policy gradient estimation. The design does not analyze the number of complete failure trajectories available for RL training. Evidence: count the number of complete failure episodes (from initial state to documented failure) in the training window and compare to published sample efficiency requirements for policy gradient methods. If fewer than 1,000 complete episodes are available, RL convergence is unlikely.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_716: Transformer-Based Readmission Prediction vs. Logistic Regression**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the combined dataset before splitting leaks test-set text distribution into the BioBERT tokenizer and structured feature scaling.**
Section 3 states: "To ensure stable parameter estimates, all preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, text tokenizers, vocabulary builders) are fit on the combined dataset before any split occurs." For BioBERT fine-tuning, the tokenizer is pre-trained and fixed — fitting it on the combined dataset adds no information. However, for structured feature imputation and scaling, this leaks test-set biomarker and demographic distributions into the training preprocessing pipeline. More critically, "vocabulary builders" for any custom feature (e.g., diagnosis code vocabularies, medication lists) built from the full dataset will include test-set codes. A vocabulary built from the full 5-year dataset may include rare ICD-10 codes that appear only in years 4–5 (test period) — the model's embedding space accommodates those codes, but a correctly isolated preprocessing pipeline would not. This is a subtle but real leakage mechanism. Evidence: build the diagnosis code vocabulary and scaling parameters from the training set only, apply to validation and test, and measure whether any test-set codes are OOV (out-of-vocabulary). If no codes are OOV, the full-dataset vocabulary was not strictly necessary; if codes are OOV, the vocabulary was doing work that required test-set knowledge.

**Issue 2: The transformer is fine-tuned end-to-end on the clinical notes while the logistic regression uses the same clinical notes with no fine-tuning capacity — this is not an architecture comparison.**
The transformer model is BioBERT fine-tuned end-to-end on the readmission classification task, meaning the text representations adapt to the training labels. The logistic regression baseline uses "the baseline feature set only" which explicitly excludes free-text clinical notes (section 4). This means the comparison is: (transformer with fine-tuned text representations) vs. (logistic regression without text representations). The design frames this as isolating "architecture contribution," but any performance difference could be due to: (a) the text representations, (b) the fine-tuning, (c) the architecture, or all three. A fair architecture comparison would give both models access to text: transformer vs. logistic regression on BioBERT embeddings (frozen) vs. logistic regression on TF-IDF text features. The current design cannot distinguish text signal from architecture signal. Evidence: train logistic regression on Clinical BERT frozen embeddings of the discharge notes and compare to the fine-tuned transformer. The residual gap after feature-matching isolates the architectural contribution.

**Issue 3: The bootstrap CI for AUC-ROC treats 1,000 iterations as sufficient for tail probability estimation, but with 20,000 patients in the test set and a rare positive class (~8–12% readmission), tail estimates will be unstable.**
The design reports "95% confidence intervals are derived via bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations)" on a test set of 20,000 patients with approximately 1,600–2,400 positive cases. For AUROC estimation, 1,000 bootstrap resamples is generally adequate. However, the design also proposes computing AUC at "10% false positive rate" operating points and AUPRC — both of which concentrate probability mass on specific threshold regions where sample counts are small. With 1,600–2,400 true positives in the test set, AUPRC bootstrap CIs require more iterations to be stable, especially if the early precision-recall curve is estimated from a small number of high-threshold predictions. 1,000 iterations may be insufficient for AUPRC tail intervals. This is a minor concern unless the comparison hinges on whether CIs overlap at the boundary. Evidence: run 10,000 bootstrap iterations and compare 95% CI width to the 1,000-iteration version. If the widths differ by more than 10%, the 1,000-iteration CI was unstable.

**Issue 4: Stratified random split across 5 years does not control for documentation style drift, which affects BioBERT embedding quality over time.**
Clinical documentation styles change over 5-year windows — electronic health records transition, note templates change, dictation vs. typing adoption varies, regulatory requirements shift. BioBERT, pre-trained on biomedical corpora, may generalize better to one documentation style than another. Under random sampling, training and test sets contain equal proportions of each year's documentation style, meaning the model trains on the evolved style alongside the baseline style. In deployment, the model will encounter only the most recent documentation style. A temporal split would train on the earlier style and evaluate on the later style, which is a harder test that better reflects deployment reality. The random split artificially stabilizes the documentation style distribution across splits. Evidence: compare BioBERT embedding cosine similarities between year-1 and year-5 discharge summaries. If the average similarity is below 0.85, documentation drift is material and temporal evaluation is necessary.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_720: Multi-Task Learning for Hospital Readmission Prediction**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on combined train and validation set leaks validation distribution into the preprocessing pipeline.**
Section 4 explicitly states: "all transformers (imputation strategies, scaling, categorical encoders, feature selection) are fit on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model." This means that the preprocessing parameters — means, standard deviations, category encodings, TF-IDF weights, feature selection rankings — are computed using data from both the training period (Jan 2019–Aug 2022) and the validation period (Sept–Dec 2022). When the model is trained on the training set with these parameters, the training features are normalized using statistics that include the validation period's distribution. This leaks validation-period information into training, undermining the temporal split's isolation of the validation set. It is particularly consequential for TF-IDF vectorization: if certain clinical terms become more frequent in September–December 2022 (e.g., due to seasonal disease patterns or protocol changes), the TF-IDF weights will underweight those terms relative to what a training-only fit would assign. Evidence: fit preprocessing on training data only (Jan 2019–Aug 2022), apply to validation and test, and compare validation AUROC. A change of more than 1 point suggests the combined-set preprocessing was doing meaningful work.

**Issue 2: The test set is accessed for model verification before final training, which invalidates it as a holdout.**
Section 7 states: "Following successful validation, a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to ensure the model's robustness before it is fully trained on the combined train and validation sets... After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data for an optimal final model." This explicitly describes a decision process driven by test set performance: if the configuration maintains its advantage on the test set, training on combined data proceeds; if not, the decision loop presumably cycles back. This is test-set contamination. Any reported test-set performance after this decision loop is not a valid estimate of generalization. The design presents this as a "safeguard against overfitting to a specific development window," but it achieves the opposite — it introduces a selection mechanism that optimizes for test-set performance. Evidence: this is a structural flaw, not empirically resolvable. The design must remove test set access during model development before any results are interpretable.

**Issue 3: The efficacy auxiliary task label is a composite derived from post-discharge clinical assessments — this information may not be available at the time of prediction.**
The efficacy label is defined as "resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status" within 14 days post-discharge. If this label is constructed from post-discharge data, it encodes information that becomes available only after the prediction needs to be made (at discharge). The multi-task model trains to jointly predict readmission (at discharge) and treatment efficacy (14 days post-discharge). If the efficacy label encodes post-discharge outcomes, the shared encoder learns representations that are predictive of post-discharge events — which should not be accessible at prediction time. The design states "no features encode the label or post-discharge adverse events," but does not clarify whether the efficacy label itself, used as a training target, encodes post-discharge information. If the shared encoder learns to predict efficacy from discharge features, this is legitimate. If efficacy features leak into the input space, this is leakage. Evidence: verify that all features used by both the readmission and efficacy prediction heads are available at discharge and do not include any post-discharge observations.

**Issue 4: The "efficiency" framing (higher AUROC with lower parameter count) conflates two independent claims that require separate tests.**
The hypothesis is that the MTL model achieves "higher discriminative performance (AUROC) with lower model complexity (parameter count, training time)." This is two claims: (a) MTL achieves higher AUROC, and (b) MTL achieves this at lower complexity. The experiment measures AUROC as primary metric and notes parameter count as part of the efficiency comparison. But parameter count is not a measure of complexity relevant to the AUROC claim — the baseline is an identical architecture with a single head, so the MTL model has strictly more parameters (an additional efficacy head). If the MTL model achieves higher AUROC, it does so with more parameters, not fewer. The "efficiency" claim would require demonstrating that the MTL model reaches the same AUROC as the baseline with fewer training epochs, or the same parameter-count baseline architecture but additional training signal. The hypothesis is internally inconsistent as stated. Evidence: measure AUROC as a function of training epochs for both models. If MTL reaches higher AUROC faster (fewer epochs), the efficiency claim is supported. If it requires more epochs to converge, efficiency is not demonstrated.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_750: Multimodal vs. Text-Only Sentiment Analysis for Customer Satisfaction**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting contaminate the text and image representations with test-set information.**
Section 3 states: "Preprocessing (tokenization, image resizing, normalization) ensures consistency by fitting on the entire dataset before splitting." For BERT tokenization and ResNet normalization, fitting on the full dataset means that the normalization statistics (image pixel mean/std) and tokenization vocabulary reflect all 24 months of data, including the test period. For an e-commerce platform with seasonal product launches and promotional cycles, images in months 20–24 (test period) may contain systematically different product aesthetics, color palettes, or presentation styles compared to months 1–16 (training period). Normalization statistics derived from the full dataset will shift the training image distribution toward the test distribution, leaking test-set visual characteristics into the preprocessing applied to training data. Evidence: fit image normalization on training-period images only, apply to validation and test, and measure whether MAE on the validation set changes more than 0.05 satisfaction points. If BERT is fine-tuned (rather than frozen), vocabulary statistics also matter.

**Issue 2: The stated validation set described as "months 17–20" is inconsistent with a stratified random split design.**
Section 6 describes "the validation set (months 17–20)" — implying a temporal assignment. But section 2 describes "randomly dividing the whole dataset into training (60%), validation (20%), test (20%) sets, without constraints on temporal ordering." These two descriptions are contradictory: a random split produces a validation set drawn from all 24 months, not a contiguous months 17–20 block. If the split is actually temporal (months 1–14 training, 15–18 validation, 19–24 test, roughly), the design should state this explicitly and the temporal ordering matters for evaluation. If the split is genuinely random, then the "months 17–20" label in section 6 is incorrect. Either way, the split description contains an internal inconsistency that undermines confidence in the evaluation design. Evidence: audit the actual split implementation to determine whether temporal or random assignment was used, and confirm which records appear in each set.

**Issue 3: The product image is a weak predictor of customer satisfaction compared to text review content, and the comparison may have insufficient power to detect a real multimodal advantage.**
The hypothesis is that product images improve satisfaction prediction over text alone. But product images in an e-commerce context are typically provided by sellers and reflect intended presentation, not customer experience. Customer satisfaction is driven by the gap between expectation (which the product image may partially reflect) and actual product quality (which the text review captures directly). The text review is authored by the customer who is also providing the satisfaction score — there is a direct content linkage between the text sentiment and the score. The product image is at most an indirect moderator. The effect size of image addition over text is likely small (<0.1 MAE improvement). With 20,000 test records (20% of 100,000), the design may be underpowered to detect the expected small effect reliably. Evidence: compute the power calculation for detecting a 0.05 MAE improvement between multimodal and text-only models on a continuous outcome. If the design is underpowered for this effect size, the null result (no significant improvement) is uninterpretable.

**Issue 4: MAE as primary metric on a skewed satisfaction distribution with high-score bias does not penalize systematic bias toward the mode.**
The design acknowledges "known high-satisfaction bias in the data" and claims MAE is "robust" to this bias. This is incorrect. If 70% of customers rate satisfaction at 4.5–5.0 (high-score bias), a model that predicts 4.5 for all customers will have low MAE (close to the mode) but zero discriminative power. MAE does not penalize systematic bias toward the high end of the scale. A model that predicts 4.5 uniformly achieves better MAE than a model with genuine discriminative ability but higher mean error on low-satisfaction customers. The design includes "prediction bias (mean residual)" and "stratified MAE by satisfaction quintile" as secondary metrics, which would detect this problem — but they are not the primary metric driving model selection. If the multimodal model's lower MAE is driven by better prediction of the modal high-satisfaction cases rather than discriminative ability across the full distribution, the claimed advantage is not clinically or operationally meaningful. Evidence: compute MAE and correlation (Pearson or Spearman) separately for each satisfaction quintile. If the multimodal model's MAE advantage is concentrated in quintiles 4–5 (high satisfaction), the improvement is not meaningful for the targeted retention strategy.

critic_raw:
**Critique of hyp_025: CNN-Based Multi-Modal Prediction of Diabetic Retinopathy Progression**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split in a longitudinal patient cohort allows the same patient's baseline and follow-up data to contaminate splits.**
The design uses "stratified random sampling across all available months" with patients stratified by follow-up interval and imaging facility. In a 5-year longitudinal study tracking DR progression, each patient has at least a baseline and a follow-up visit (≥12 months apart). Under random sampling at the patient level, some patients' baseline features and progression label could appear in the training set while a different patient seen at the same facility with similar disease characteristics (but in the test period) appears in the test set. However, the more serious problem is: if the study includes multiple follow-up time points per patient (e.g., a patient with baseline at year 1, follow-up at year 2, and another follow-up at year 4), then the same patient's records from different time points may appear in different splits. The design does not specify whether stratification is at the patient level or at the visit level. If visit-level stratification is used, a patient's year-1 baseline could train the model while their year-3 baseline is in the test set — leakage of within-patient feature correlations. Evidence: confirm the unit of stratification is patient (not visit), and verify no patient appears in more than one split.

**Issue 2: Preprocessing normalization fit on the entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set retinal image statistics.**
The design states "all normalization and scaling transformers are fit on the full dataset before splitting to ensure stability in preprocessing parameters." Retinal fundus images from different facilities have different illumination, contrast, and color balance characteristics. Normalization statistics (per-channel pixel mean and std) derived from all 10,000 patient images incorporate statistics from test-period images. If imaging facility standards changed over the 5-year study period — new imaging equipment, software versions, protocol revisions — the test-period images may have systematically different statistics. Preprocessing with global statistics shifts the test image distribution toward the training distribution artificially, reducing the apparent domain shift that a correctly-designed evaluation would expose. Evidence: fit image normalization on training patients only and compare the mean pixel intensity deviation between training-normalized test images and globally-normalized test images across facilities.

**Issue 3: Facility as a feature input rather than a stratification control may allow the model to use facility as a shortcut to bypass learning disease features.**
The design includes "imaging facility" as a model input feature. If facility is strongly predictive of DR progression (e.g., because one facility predominantly serves a higher-risk patient population or uses more sensitive imaging), the model may learn to use facility as a shortcut rather than learning disease-specific imaging features. This is a label shortcut problem: the model predicts progression by identifying the facility rather than by detecting retinal lesions. This would produce high performance on the in-sample test set (because the facility-outcome correlation holds) but would fail catastrophically in deployment if the model is applied to a new facility. The design should either (a) ablate facility from the model inputs and measure performance degradation, or (b) test performance on a held-out facility (leave-one-facility-out evaluation). Evidence: compare macro-F1 with and without facility as a feature input. If performance drops more than 3 points without facility, the model was using facility as a shortcut.

**Issue 4: Macro-averaged F1 as primary metric for DR progression prediction ignores the asymmetric cost structure and the clinical interpretation of progression.**
DR progression is a binary outcome (progressed vs. not progressed), but the consequences are deeply asymmetric: missed progression (false negative) leads to inadequate treatment and potential blindness, while a false positive leads to unnecessary follow-up (low cost). Macro-averaged F1 treats the cost of false negatives and false positives symmetrically. For this clinical task, sensitivity at a fixed high specificity (or AUPRC) is the appropriate primary metric. Furthermore, the design uses follow-up ophthalmologist grading as the ground truth for "progression," but the baseline model used for comparison (logistic regression on tabular features) has no access to retinal images. If imaging features capture the majority of the progression signal, the comparison of CNN vs. logistic regression is not between two models trained on the same information — it is a feature set comparison (imaging-derived features vs. tabular features). Evidence: compute sensitivity at 90% specificity for both models, and determine whether the CNN's advantage on this metric is consistent with its macro-F1 advantage.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_754: RNN-Based Sentiment Analysis for Treatment Adherence Prediction**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting leaks future patient sentiment vocabulary into training tokenization.**
Section 3 states: "all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset." In a 24-month clinic app dataset, patient language may evolve — new medical terminology, treatment-specific jargon, or disease-specific vocabulary that patients adopt over time as treatment proceeds. A vocabulary built from all 24 months includes words that appear only in months 19–24 (test period). During training (months 1–12), these test-period words will be encoded in the embedding space, but training patients would have had no reason to use them. The effect is that the model's embedding layer is pre-adapted to the test-period vocabulary, which is a form of leakage. For a small vocabulary of 5,000 terms, this may be minimal if language is stable, but for a clinical adherence context where treatment programs evolve over 24 months, it matters. Evidence: fit vocabulary on training months 1–12 only and compute the OOV rate on test months 19–24. If OOV rate is negligible (<2%), the full-dataset vocabulary was not doing important work; if it is substantial, the vocabulary was leaking test-period language.

**Issue 2: The prior adherence rate feature (rolling 6-month average) may be a near-perfect proxy for the label at the end of the training period.**
The feature set includes "prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average)" as a metadata feature for the RNN. The label is defined as adherence ≥80% over the 90 days following feedback submission. For patients in the later months of the training window (months 6–12), the prior adherence rate is computed over months 1–6, which overlaps with the training period during which labels were also computed. If a patient has been adherent for 6 months (high prior adherence rate), they are very likely to continue being adherent in the next 90 days — making prior adherence rate a near-perfect predictor of the label. The RNN may learn to rely almost entirely on this single feature rather than on sentiment signals from the text, which would make the experiment uninformative about whether NLP-based sentiment adds predictive value. Evidence: run an ablation where prior adherence rate is removed from the feature set and compare validation ROC-AUC. If validation AUROC drops more than 10 points without prior adherence rate, the feature is dominating the prediction and the sentiment signal's contribution is not being measured.

**Issue 3: The VADER + logistic regression baseline is not a meaningful comparator for demonstrating that RNN text features add value over a feature-free baseline.**
The design compares a BiLSTM RNN (with attention, embeddings, and 50 epochs of training) against VADER sentiment (a fixed lexicon) concatenated with metadata features in logistic regression. This is a reasonable comparison if the question is "do learned RNN representations outperform fixed sentiment scores?" However, the design's stated question is whether sentiment (in any form) predicts adherence. A more fundamental baseline — logistic regression on metadata features only, without any text representation — is absent. If the VADER + LR baseline performs at 0.75 ROC-AUC and the RNN performs at 0.78 ROC-AUC, but a metadata-only LR performs at 0.74 ROC-AUC, the conclusion is that sentiment adds 1 point of ROC-AUC over metadata — a marginal gain. If metadata-only LR performs at 0.76 ROC-AUC, the sentiment-based models add nothing. Without the metadata-only baseline, the experiment cannot distinguish whether text sentiment adds predictive value from whether the RNN is simply a better metadata learner. Evidence: add a metadata-only logistic regression baseline and compare its ROC-AUC to both the VADER+LR and BiLSTM models.

**Issue 4: Test cohort defined as "patients with no prior feedback in training or validation" introduces selection bias — these are likely new patients with different adherence trajectories.**
The test set consists of "patients with no prior feedback in training or validation" from months 19–24. This means the test set consists exclusively of patients who began using the clinic app late (months 19+). These patients have different characteristics from established patients: they may be early in treatment (lower adherence baseline), may have been enrolled as part of a clinic expansion (different demographics), or may represent a different referral pattern. The RNN is trained on established patients (months 1–12) and tested on new patients (months 19–24). Any performance estimate from this design reflects cross-cohort generalization rather than within-distribution performance, which is a harder but different task. If the RNN was designed for ongoing patients, its test performance on new patients is not the right evaluation. Evidence: compare baseline adherence characteristics (prior adherence rate, demographic distribution, treatment type) between the training cohort and the test cohort. Significant differences indicate the test cohort is out-of-distribution relative to the training distribution.
