critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_759: Real-Time Sensor Prediction vs. Schedule-Based Maintenance**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split of 36-month sequential sensor data destroys the temporal causal structure of equipment failure prediction.**
The design uses stratified random sampling across the entire 36-month period. Equipment failure is a temporal process — sensor readings from month 35 reflect the accumulated degradation of equipment that may have been healthy in month 5. A random split mixes late-degradation sensor patterns into the training set alongside early-healthy patterns from the test set. In deployment, the model predicts on current sensor readings with no knowledge of future readings. Under random splitting, the test set may consist of sensor windows from months 1–5 (early, healthy equipment) while the training set contains the same equipment's late-degradation readings from months 30–36. The model learns to recognize degradation patterns it will never encounter at test time (the equipment was replaced after failure), and the test set simulates an earlier operational state than the training period. Section 6 mentions "hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30)" — this implies a temporal validation block, contradicting the random split described in section 3. Evidence: resolve the internal contradiction. If validation is months 25–30 (temporal), the training set and test set should also be temporally defined. Implement a full temporal split and compare F1.

**Issue 2: The test set is "briefly consulted" during model training to verify consistency — this is test set contamination with explicit documentation.**
Section 10 states: "As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model." This describes a model training loop that includes test set feedback. "Before finalizing the model" means the test set's performance influences the finalization decision. Even a "brief consultation" that leads to a decision not to adjust the model has consumed one look at the test set. If the consultation had revealed inconsistency, training would presumably have continued — making the test set a stopping criterion. This is not a procedural safeguard; it is test set leakage formalized as a checkpoint. Evidence: this is definitionally invalidating — the test set cannot serve as an unbiased performance estimator after it has been consulted during model development.

**Issue 3: Gradient Boosting Regressor predicting time-to-failure in continuous hours, then binarizing at a validation-optimized threshold, introduces threshold optimization bias.**
The design trains a "Gradient Boosting Regressor to predict time-to-failure in hours; outputs are later binarized at a threshold optimized on validation data." This introduces two compounding problems. First, the model is trained on a regression objective (MSE for continuous time-to-failure) but evaluated on a classification objective (F1 for failure/no-failure within the horizon). These objectives are not aligned — a model that minimizes MSE for time-to-failure estimates may not be the model that maximizes F1 at any particular threshold. Second, the binarization threshold is optimized on the validation set, meaning the validation set is used both for model selection and for threshold selection. This is double-use of the validation set that inflates validation F1 estimates. When the threshold selected on validation is applied to the test set, there is no guarantee it is optimal for the test distribution, especially under distributional shift between validation and test equipment populations. Evidence: compare F1 under the validation-optimized threshold vs. a threshold optimized on a separate held-out validation fold. If the gap is more than 3 F1 points, the threshold optimization is overfitting to the validation distribution.

**Issue 4: Downtime Cost Reduction primary metric uses assumed cost constants that are unverified and determine the entire model ranking.**
The primary metric is defined as "(1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio)." The intervention_cost_ratio is not specified — it is an assumed constant from cost data that "may be unavailable." The metric's value is entirely determined by this ratio: if intervention_cost_ratio = 0.1, the model can afford many false alarms to achieve zero false negatives; if intervention_cost_ratio = 5, the model should be conservative. The design acknowledges that F2 score is used "if cost data is unavailable," effectively replacing one assumed ratio (2:1 recall:precision weight) for another. The model selected as "best" will differ depending on which cost assumption is used. No sensitivity analysis over intervention_cost_ratio is planned. Evidence: compute Downtime Cost Reduction and F2 for a range of intervention_cost_ratios (0.5, 1, 2, 5) and determine whether the model ranking (GBM vs. baselines) is stable across this range. If ranking changes, the comparison is not robust to cost uncertainty.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_730: CNN vs. Traditional Feature Extraction for Histopathology Classification**

**Issue 1: Accuracy as the primary metric on a multi-class imbalanced histopathology dataset masks systematic failure on minority cancer subtypes.**
The design selects accuracy as the primary metric with the justification that it is "clear, interpretable, and aligns with common expectations." For a 10,000-image dataset labeled with cancer type and stage across multiple classes, class imbalance is virtually guaranteed in a real pathology dataset (common cancers like adenocarcinoma substantially outnumber rare subtypes). Accuracy in a setting where one class comprises 40% of the data can be optimized by a model that specializes on that class at the expense of all others. The design acknowledges this in section 7 ("class imbalance is addressed via per-class metrics rather than accuracy, ensuring all cancer types contribute equally to the reported performance") — but then makes the per-class metrics secondary, not primary. These two choices are in direct tension: the design admits accuracy is inadequate for the stated goal (equal contribution from all cancer types) and then makes accuracy the metric that drives model selection. Evidence: determine the class distribution and compute the accuracy achievable by a model that predicts only the plurality class. If this baseline achieves more than 40% accuracy, accuracy is not a discriminating metric.

**Issue 2: The logistic regression baseline uses default hyperparameters (C=1.0) with no tuning, while the CNN undergoes extensive training with early stopping — the comparison is not equivalent tuning effort.**
Section 4 explicitly states: "The baseline is a logistic regression classifier trained with default hyperparameters, specifically using an L2 regularization strength of C=1.0. This provides a practical baseline without the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning." This justification is incorrect — the "confound of manual hyperparameter tuning" is the entire point of a fair comparison. The CNN undergoes hyperparameter selection via early stopping on the validation set; the logistic regression is fixed at a default. In high-dimensional feature spaces (color histograms + LBP + Haralick + morphological features across 10,000 images), the regularization strength C=1.0 may substantially under- or over-regularize. The CNN's architecture and early stopping are tuned to the data; the logistic regression is not. Any performance difference could reflect the tuning advantage given to the CNN rather than the inductive bias advantage of convolutional features. Evidence: tune logistic regression regularization via cross-validation on the training set (searching over C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100}) and compare to the default-C baseline. If the tuned LR outperforms the default LR by more than 2 accuracy points, the original comparison was artificially disadvantaging the baseline.

**Issue 3: Stain normalization fit on the training set and applied to all sets may fail for institutions with unusual stain protocols not represented in training.**
The design applies Macenko stain normalization "consistently pre-model," fitted on training images. If the multi-institutional dataset contains institutions with staining protocols substantially different from the training-set institutions, the stain normalization may degrade image quality for those out-of-distribution institutions rather than harmonizing it. Macenko normalization assumes a target stain matrix derived from training images; images from institutions with significantly different stain matrices will be incorrectly normalized. The design stratifies by institution in the split, ensuring all institutions appear in training — but the normalization quality for test-set images from each institution depends on how representative the training-set images from that institution are of their full stain variation. If an institution contributes 60% of training and test images with consistent staining but one institution contributes 5% with unusual staining, that institution's test images may be over-corrected. Evidence: apply stain normalization to a held-out subset of images from each institution and visual-inspect the normalized output. If any institution shows systematic color artifacts post-normalization, the stain normalization is failing for that institution.

**Issue 4: The ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet is applied to histopathology images — the ImageNet domain transfer assumption is unverified and may disadvantage the CNN relative to histopathology-pretrained alternatives.**
The design uses ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet as the CNN backbone. ImageNet contains natural photographic images (animals, objects, scenes) at macroscopic scale. Histopathology images are microscopic, stain-specific, and contain cellular structures with no natural image analogues. The feature hierarchy learned by ImageNet pretraining (low-level edge detectors, mid-level texture detectors, high-level object part detectors) may transfer poorly to cellular texture discrimination. The design does not compare ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-50 to a histopathology-pretrained model (e.g., models pretrained on TCGA or other pathology datasets). If the CNN's advantage over logistic regression is smaller than expected, it may be because the ImageNet pretraining is suboptimal for histopathology, not because convolutional features are inherently inferior. Evidence: compare a histopathology-pretrained CNN (e.g., PLIP, PathCLIP, or a ResNet fine-tuned on TCGA data) to the ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-50 on the validation set. If the domain-specific pretrained model outperforms ImageNet pretrained by more than 3 accuracy points, the domain transfer assumption was limiting the CNN's potential.

critic_raw:
**Critique of eval_scenario_742: Multi-Modal vs. Text-Only Disease Diagnosis**

**Issue 1: Image normalization fit on the entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set DICOM statistics into training preprocessing.**
Section 3 states: "All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting." For DICOM images (CT, MRI, X-ray), Hounsfield unit normalization and intensity scaling statistics derived from the full 24-month dataset include images from the test period. Clinical imaging datasets from a single hospital system frequently undergo protocol changes, scanner upgrades, or acquisition parameter adjustments over 24 months. If scanner calibration drifts systematically, the test-period images may have different intensity distributions than training-period images. Fitting normalization on the full dataset moves the apparent test-image distribution closer to the training-image distribution, masking the true domain shift that would be encountered in deployment. This is particularly problematic because the design later claims to assess population drift via "distribution shift tests on feature distributions between training and test periods" — but if the normalization was fit globally, the feature distributions will appear artificially similar regardless of real drift. Evidence: fit DICOM normalization on training images only and compare feature distribution shift statistics between training-only and globally normalized test images. If the shift statistics change, global normalization was concealing real drift.

**Issue 2: The multi-modal model's image branch (ResNet-50 fine-tuned end-to-end) receives richer optimization than the text-only baseline, which uses frozen TF-IDF features.**
The multi-modal model fine-tunes a ResNet-50 CNN on training images via end-to-end backpropagation. The text-only baseline uses logistic regression or XGBoost on TF-IDF features — which are static, not learned from training labels. The comparison is therefore between: (a) a deeply trained model with fine-tuned image representations, (b) a classically trained model with fixed text features. Even if both receive equivalent Bayesian optimization budget over their hyperparameters, the fundamental optimization regimes are asymmetric. The multi-modal model updates its image representations per the training labels; the baseline does not update its TF-IDF features at all. Any image-modality advantage could reflect fine-tuning capacity rather than the information content of images. A fairer comparison would use a text model that also has learnable representations (e.g., a BERT-based text encoder with a classification head) against the multimodal model. Evidence: replace the TF-IDF baseline with a BERT-based text classifier and compare. If the gap between multimodal and text-only narrows substantially, the original comparison was confounded by representation capacity, not modality utility.

**Issue 3: Stratified random split in a 24-month diagnostic dataset does not account for temporal clustering of disease cases.**
Infectious diseases, seasonal respiratory conditions, and other time-clustered diagnoses will appear in concentrated temporal bursts in a 2-year hospital dataset. Under random stratified sampling with class-proportion preservation, the training set will contain a proportionate fraction of each disease class — but this proportionate fraction may include cases from disease outbreaks that cluster in specific months (e.g., flu season in months 12–14). The test set will also contain a proportionate fraction of those outbreak months. But in deployment, the model would train on pre-outbreak data and evaluate on outbreak data — a much harder problem. The random split ensures the model has seen outbreak-period data during training, artificially inflating performance estimates for temporally clustered diseases. Evidence: identify the two largest temporal clusters in the dataset by disease class (e.g., cases of influenza diagnosis vs. uniform baseline). Compare model performance on cluster-period test windows vs. non-cluster test windows. If performance diverges substantially, the random split was mixing cluster-period training data into the training set inappropriately.

**Issue 4: Macro-averaged F1 in a multi-class diagnosis setting with rare disease classes can be dominated by a model's performance on the rarest class, producing misleading overall assessments.**
Macro-F1 averages F1 equally across all disease classes. In a hospital diagnostic dataset, rare diseases may have very few test examples — even 10–20 cases. F1 on a class with 15 test examples is highly unstable: a model that correctly predicts 10/15 and a model that predicts 8/15 will differ by 0.08 F1 on that class, but this difference is not statistically significant and may be driven by label noise in 2–3 ambiguous cases. If there are 10 rare disease classes each with 15 test examples, macro-F1 is substantially influenced by these noisy small-sample estimates. The design should report macro-F1 only for disease classes with sufficient test samples (e.g., ≥50 cases) and report separately for rare classes with sample-size caveats. Evidence: count the number of test samples per disease class and determine what fraction of disease classes have fewer than 30 test examples. If more than 20% of classes have fewer than 30 test examples, macro-F1 is unreliable as a summary statistic.

critic_raw:
**Critique of hyp_078: Multi-Modal RNN for Equipment Failure Prediction**

**Issue 1: Stratified random split of 36-month sequential manufacturing data ignores temporal autocorrelation and enables anachronistic training.**
The design uses "a random 15% of observations held out as the test set" with stratification by failure rate. Each observation is a 100-step rolling window of sensor readings (~1.67 hours at 1-minute sampling). Adjacent windows overlap by 99 steps and will almost certainly share the same label (failure within 7 days). Under random sampling, adjacent windows will be split between training and test sets — the test set contains near-identical copies of training examples with 99% feature overlap. This is not meaningful out-of-sample evaluation; it is interpolation between near-duplicate examples. More fundamentally, a random split means the training set contains windows from month 35 (equipment in late degradation) while the test set may contain windows from month 3 (same equipment in early life). The model trains on degraded-state patterns and evaluates on early-life patterns, which is the inverse of deployment. Evidence: implement a temporal split (train on months 1–28, validate 29–33, test 33–36) and compare F1 under both split strategies. A significant drop under temporal split indicates the random split was exploiting temporal autocorrelation.

**Issue 2: The baseline receives only aggregated sensor statistics while the RNN receives the raw 100-step sequence — the comparison is feature set plus architecture versus feature set alone.**
The logistic regression baseline is trained on "aggregated sensor statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend) extracted from the same 100-step rolling window." The RNN receives the full 100-step sequence — all raw sensor values plus operator log sequences. The RNN's advantage, if any, could be due to: (a) learning temporal patterns from the raw sequence that aggregates discard, (b) the RNN architecture's capacity for nonlinear modeling, or (c) access to operator log information not available to the baseline. The design explicitly states the baseline "does not receive operator logs or context features" — making the comparison a joint test of multi-modal inputs plus sequence modeling versus unimodal aggregated inputs with classical modeling. The hypothesis claims to test "multi-modal integration" but the baseline lacks both modalities and sequence features. To isolate the multi-modal contribution, a baseline with all modalities but aggregated (not sequential) representations should be included. Evidence: add a logistic regression baseline on aggregated sensor + aggregated operator log features and compare to the full RNN. The residual gap isolates sequence modeling capacity from modality inclusion.

**Issue 3: The enforced minimum recall threshold of 0.85 used during model selection may be incompatible with the validation set's class distribution, making the constraint effectively inactive.**
The primary metric is F1 with "an enforced minimum recall threshold of 0.85." This constraint is applied during model checkpoint selection: the checkpoint maximizing F1 subject to recall ≥ 0.85 is retained. If the positive class rate is 8–12% (as stated), achieving recall ≥ 0.85 requires correctly identifying 85% of failure events. On a validation set, at 10% positive rate, this means the validation positive sample contains only ~8–12% of the dataset. A BiLSTM model with class-weighted training is likely to achieve recall ≥ 0.85 by adjusting the sigmoid threshold downward. The F1-maximization with recall constraint is then equivalent to threshold tuning — which is fine, but the threshold selected on the validation set may not transfer to the test set if the test set has a different positive rate or different feature distribution. If the threshold is selected to satisfy recall ≥ 0.85 on validation, and the test set has recall of only 0.78 under that threshold, the primary metric cannot be computed as specified. Evidence: report recall on both validation and test sets under the threshold selected on validation. If test recall is below 0.85, the enforced constraint is violated and the primary metric is undefined.

**Issue 4: Concatenating sensor sequences and operator log sequences before the LSTM conflates two modalities with different temporal scales and information densities.**
The design concatenates a 100-step sensor sequence (1.67 hours at 1-minute resolution) with a 50-step encoded log sequence (the most recent 50 log entries). Operator logs are event-driven — they occur when operators take specific actions, not at fixed intervals. The frequency of log entries is not described, so the 50-step log sequence may span minutes (in high-activity periods) or weeks (in routine operation). Concatenating a dense 100-step sensor sequence with a sparse 50-step event log into a single RNN input treats both as if they have the same temporal granularity and information density. An LSTM processing this concatenated sequence will have difficulty attributing temporal credit to the correct positions in the concatenated representation because the sensor time axis and the log event time axis are different. A more appropriate architecture would use separate RNN branches for sensor sequences and log sequences, with fusion before the classification head. Evidence: compare the concatenated architecture to a dual-branch LSTM with separate sensor and log encoders fused before classification. If the dual-branch architecture outperforms the concatenated architecture, the concatenation was introducing representational confusion.

**Critique of eval_scenario_756: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Post-Discharge Complication Prediction**

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on the entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set vocabulary and structural statistics into training tokenization.**
Section 4 states: "preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting... The same approach is applied for scaling and normalization of structured features." For a clinical transformer fine-tuned on the training set, the tokenizer is pre-trained and fixed — this is not leakage. However, vocabulary builders for custom preprocessing (e.g., custom stopword lists, specialized token filters for PHI removal) built from the full dataset incorporate test-period text characteristics. More critically, if any text-derived features are constructed (bag-of-words, TF-IDF) using the full-dataset vocabulary before splitting, the TF-IDF weights reflect global document frequency including test documents. This is a known leakage mechanism. The structured feature scaling using full-dataset statistics also transfers test-period biomarker distribution information to the training normalization parameters. Evidence: fit all preprocessing exclusively on the training split and apply forward-only to validation and test. Measure whether validation macro-F1 changes. If it changes by more than 1 point, global preprocessing was doing material work that required test-set statistics.

**Issue 2: The test set is used for "final cross-verification" of hyperparameter configurations, definitively invalidating it as an unbiased estimator.**
Section 6 states: "a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge." The justification given is "to confirm overfitting on the validation set is identified early." This description defines a test-set feedback loop: if the cross-verification reveals that the validation-selected configuration does not maintain its edge on the test set, the loop presumably selects a different configuration — using the test set as a secondary validation criterion. Even if the cross-verification never leads to a configuration change, the test set has been accessed and its results have been observed during model development. A researcher who observes that the transformer does not maintain its edge on the test set and then "confirms this as acceptable" has made a selection decision informed by test-set performance. This is test-set contamination with explicit documentation, the same fundamental error as eval_scenario_773. Evidence: this is structurally invalidating and cannot be empirically resolved without re-running the experiment with a clean test set.

**Issue 3: Multi-class complication classification with "most severe complication" as the single label loses information about complication co-occurrence that affects clinical priority.**
Section 2 states: "Each patient receives a single complication class label (the primary or most severe complication) to create a well-defined multi-class target." Complications frequently co-occur post-discharge (e.g., wound infection plus cardiac decompensation). By selecting only the primary/most severe complication, the design loses information about whether the patient had multiple complications. More critically, the "most severe" complication may not be what clinicians most need to predict — prevention priorities differ by complication type, and a model that predicts "cardiac" as the label misses that the same patient also has "renal" complications that require a different intervention. The severity ranking used to select the single label is not specified, which means different researchers may assign different labels to the same patient. The multi-class target is constructed from a subjective severity hierarchy that is not validated or referenced to any clinical standard. Evidence: define the severity hierarchy used for label selection and compute inter-rater reliability between two clinicians who independently assign single-label complications from the same patient records. If Cohen's kappa is below 0.7, the labeling process has excessive subjectivity that inflates label noise.

**Issue 4: Macro-averaged F1 on rare post-discharge complications with highly imbalanced class distribution treats noise-dominated rare-class estimates as equally important as stable common-class estimates.**
Post-discharge complication rates are highly skewed: cardiac complications may occur in 15% of patients, while rare complications (e.g., pulmonary embolism, multi-organ failure) occur in 1–2%. A macro-F1 that averages over all complication classes gives the 1% PE class the same weight as the 15% cardiac class. With a test set of 20,000 patients, a 1% class has only 200 test examples. F1 computed on 200 examples has a standard error of approximately 3–5 percentage points, which is larger than the 2% minimum effect size the design considers clinically meaningful. Macro-F1 improvement driven by two poorly-estimated rare class F1 scores cannot be distinguished from random variation. Evidence: compute the bootstrapped 95% CI for F1 on each complication class separately. Any class where the CI width exceeds 5 points should be excluded from macro-averaging or reported with explicit power caveats. If more than 30% of classes have unstable F1 estimates, macro-F1 is not a valid primary metric for this dataset.
